sentences.text: The train was pulling out, and we were just in the open country when two American fighter bombers attacked the train.
The two engines got shot out, and a number of SS and some prisoners got killed.
Then the German guards jumped right off the train and surrounded the whole train so that nobody should escape.
And then we were sitting there for-- the Americans dropped some bombs on the town.
The town was called [?
Reitzenheim. ?] And we tried to get away from this,and the population came right away and were very hostile.
Sudeten Germans.
And-- and the SS.
Some Sudeten Germans came right away out.
And we were-- had to pick up a few SS men to take him to a-- a hospital, who got wounded.
When he came back, the Germans were separating the healthy ones from the sick ones already.
We had to line up.
And then a car with six men came on with machine pistols.
And they start to open fire right away at the-- at the sick ones.
Then they picked out maybe a dozen fellows and told them, you stay here.
You throw the bodies in the box car.
And then I was marched off towards Czechoslovakia.
We were marched in groups of 200.
And there were maybe 25 SS men in the-- guarding the 200. 10 on each side and five in the back.
And after a while, we heard some shooting.
Anyone who fell behind or could not keep up got shot.
Into Czechoslovakia.
We were told that.
And in the first night, we were in a factory.
And there was a house nearby, and some fellows stole a few potatoes because we had nothing to eat.
And the woman caught one of them, and she reported it to an SS man, and the fellow was shot right away.
And then we marched for a couple of days.
And wherever we went through the village which were German, the [GERMAN] was the German old people standing there with rifle and watching, making sure that nobody could escape.
When we went to districts which were Czech, we found bread and things on the road.
We could--
Yeah.
We could pick up.
Then on-- I think on the third or fourth day, we arrived in Leitmeritz, in the camp.
A small camp, mostly for Czechs.
Close to 500.
We left over 1,500 in-- in [GERMAN] when we left.
So a few escaped, which I found out later on.
But I would say 1,000 got-- at least 1,000 got shot.
We stayed overnight in the camp on the camp ground.
Next morning, we were called up for roll call, and all Jews step out.
So we had no other choice, step over to the side.
And then we were marched to Theresienstadt.
Theresienstadt was a-- a small village which was originally [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] built by Maria Theresa as a fortress in the-- in the-- in the 1800s.
And it was a-- a garrison for the Czech army.
And the Germans took everyone out and made it a concentration camp.
Now, there were a few camps in Theresienstadt, the big camp and the-- the-- and the little camp.
The old fortress--
We came in the old fortress.
And when we arrived, we were put right away in isolation, not to have contact with anyone, and they gave us a piece of bread.
Other Jews, yes.
Only Jews were there.
Not that day, none.
And then later on in the afternoon, we got marched to one of the barracks which was called the [GERMAN].
And we were put with 25 in one room to sleep on the floor.
Hamburg is a city.
[GERMAN] means barrack of Hamburg.
Barrack of [GERMAN].
Barrack of [GERMAN].
You know, so far.
That's how they were named.
Who named them, I don't know.
Because Theresienstadt was a-- a ghetto, which I heard later on, that over 80,000 Jews from Theresienstadt were sent to Auschwitz.
And she said, well, they all went to Auschwitz.
And we started laughing because nobody in Theresienstadt knew what Auschwitz was.
We knew.
We didn't tell them anything the first day.
We had no contact with except for few Czechs who run the barracks.
And they were no angels.
Who-- who were in charge there.
They were stealing food from us.
We found that out afterwards.
And then we had a number of death on the first night, on the-- bodies were swarming the hallways.
Some even throw the bloody folk the balcony of the third floor right to the ground.
The bodies were-- I don't know they were moved the next day or not.
I don't know.
Anyway, they threw them in-- in one of the build-- down behind one of the buildings.
No.
Because a few days later, I started to work at the crematoria.
That's how I knew there was nobody buried.
We-- we knew the war would go to end because we had-- we knew the Americans were coming in the middle of Germany already when we-- when they moved us out.
But in Theresienstadt, we were isolated.
And the people in Theresienstadt knew nothing--
--comparison.
Nothing there.
And then we were kept isolated in those barracks.
And a few days later, they came along and they wanted some people to work.
You would get double rations, they said.
So another fellow I was together with in Jungfernhof said, hey, let's go.
What can be so bad?
Here we look at four walls and if they're not give us anything to eat, maybe we get something to eat.
Yeah.
But what we did was unloading bodies from the box cars.
Where were the box cars coming from?
From the outside.
Prisoners the Germans were-- dead prisoners or some were alive, they were shipping in.
On the-- were 50 to 60 in a box car, up to 40 box cars at a time.
Well, since I had handled bodies in Theresienstadt, so it didn't bother me.
The only argument I had was-- was with a doctor.
He said, I have to look at the bodies first before you can take them out.
Only it took him too long.
And I said to him, look.
They smell already.
Anything move, you can have.
What doesn't move is go on the wagon, because we are not sitting here all night waiting for you.
And I did that every day till-- for a week, maybe.
Then they took me to work in a crematoria on and off.
Till July 11.
I was-- I-- when the Russians took-- came in Theresienstadt, those who worked in the crematoria, also some others who were in charge, they all run away.
They were Czechs.
And so I got stuck with it.
Yes.
The Russians said, you are free.
But 24 hours later, they arrested us and they-- we had to go back to work.
And they said, you are volunteers from now on.
Because you have typhus fever in the camp, and if you cannot control it you all go through the smokestack.
Back to work.
Yes.
You have to go back to work.
You are important person here.
Yeah.
July 11.
Then the epidemic was down.
There were very few who died on-- Germans prisoners were doing the work because I was supervising them already for quite a while.
So they said I can go home.
They two-- gave me two choices.
I could stay in Czechoslovakia for the service I had rendered with the typhus epidemic, or I could go to Germany.
But they said, there's nothing in Germany.
I'm better off to stay here.
But since the Russians had so well-behaved and so much trouble was in Theresienstadt, the combination, especially by Russian citizens.
Because were some things.
The Latvian Jews blamed the German Jews for the massacres of the Germans-- of the Latvians in 1941 because they killed the Latvians to make room for the Germans.
Some Latvian Jews-- who spoke Russian, said we are Russian citizen-- were accusing German Jews of collaboration.
The Russians arrested some, and some had to run.
And also I saw quite a few things which never should have happened.
The delousing process was going too slow, even though there was more clothing than they even could handle.
But nobody--
After liberation.
Till the Americans shipped in DDT.
Then they got rid of the lice.
Not before.
Thousands died which had-- did not have to die if they would have put a little [INAUDIBLE] behind.
Railroad trains.
None.
I had a repatriation slip which gave me free ride by the railroad.
The Czech government.
That the hometown-- the church was bombed and nothing else.
And I thought-- I-- I hope all those Nazis are around so I can knock a few teeth out.
I was about 23.
What did you find when you saw your home?
First-- well, I-- I was not feeling well.
And I could not walk any stairs.
So I told-- I met a friend.
We said-- he said, stay with me.
I said, no.
I'm going to hospital.
Something is wrong with me.
And I went to the hospital, and I had a blood pressure under 90.
Yes.
Because I had gained too much weight--
--after the liberation and retained the body fluid.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was up to over 180 pounds.
Nobody.
There were no Jews left.
And I found out when they got deported and where and everything.
Riga.
With Riga.
The rest went up to Theresienstadt.
Because they were only old people.
My father got liberated.
He was in-- worked for the army.
The [SPEAKING GERMAN].
That means army clothing office 701.
And he got shipped from Riga to Libau, and from Libau to Hamburg.
He was there in the central prison.
From there to the concentration camp in [GERMAN].
And then Count Bernadotte came into the act with him now.
And he got turned over on May 1 to the Swedish government.
And they were sent to Sweden with the rest of the family.
Yes.
In December, 1947.
Yes.
I found out that they were alive towards the end of 1945.
Because somebody got a newspaper from England.
The names were listed there, who went to Sweden.
Because there was no mail you can send outside of Germany or coming in.
Yes.
I had no other choice.
I stayed in the hospital for six months.
Yes.
I throw the Nazi out of the house, which he had owned, and I took an apartment in a house.
Yes.
Yes.
I could not reclaim it in the beginning.
Later on, I did.
We knew there were trials going on, but it was very difficult to travel from one zone to the other.
And--
British.
And the British were a little bit anti-Jewish also, sorry to say.
Because a little trouble was going on in Palestine.
A friend-- a friend saw a British newspaper where the name of my family was in.
But there was a-- the newspaper were printing about the Nuremberg Trials, which was on the censorship of the military government.
No.
It was this way.
My brother married to a girl from Lodz in Sweden in 1947.
Early '47.
And he wrote me that-- then the mail was going-- that his brother-in-law is in Furth.
That's next to Nuremberg.
If I could visit him and see what he's doing.
And I wrote back, yeah.
I will go there.
And I contact this fellow that I would go there.
And then we had a meeting of the survivors in Dusseldorf.
When I went to the meeting, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they were all talking about going to Palestine.
It was the last thing I wanted to do.
So I left the meeting and I visited a cousin of my brother-in-law.
And here was a-- a girl in uniform.
First, he wouldn't let me in.
And then when finally he talked to her, and I-- and I said, I have to go to Furth next week.
We were talking about the cousin.
She said, I'm stationed in Nuremberg.
And then as joke, I said, hey, that we can go out.
Have a good time.
And she answered, but-- yeah.
But don't have any ideas.
I made a date with her.
In Nuremberg.
And then she said towards me, I have to go in a courtroom.
We cannot go in the afternoon because the interpreter's sick and I have to translate.
And another girl said, why don't you give him a pass to go in the-- there in that trial, because it is-- it is fascinating.
So they-- yeah.
We can do that.
So they got me a pass to go in the courtroom.
Then I hear the-- a fellow talking about the murders in Eastern Europe.
And the guy is talking and talking and talking.
And I say, hey.
The guy is talking baloney.
I don't know who it was.
This is all wrong.
And we-- he was talking to, I know the fellow that was Eduard Strauch.
He was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a major or-- or-- or [GERMAN] in the SS.
The butcher from Minsk.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So after a while, the girl comes, and I tell her, listen.
What's going on in the courtroom?
Who is that joker that's the prosecutor?
Why?
So I tell her, he is wrong.
So and so.
The ghetto got liquidated then, then, and then, and this, this, and this.
You sure?
I says, look.
I pick up the clothing when I worked in the motor pool.
She said, wait a minute.
She goes over and knocks on the wood there where the separation is.
And calls the prosecutor over.
And tells that to the prosecutor.
And the prosecutor said, wait a minute.
How do you know that?
Well, a friend just told me.
And he said, right.
Was your friend in the SS?
And she said, no.
He is a survivor.
He is Jewish.
You want to talk to him?
So you went to meet the prosecutor?
So I meet the prosecutor.
Benjamin Ferencz.
What I knew about Jost.
What I knew about Sandberger.
And what I knew about Strauch.
All of the mass killings, what I have witnesses and what I know about.
He said, are you sure?
I said, yes, I am sure.
So and so and so.
I worked in the motor pool for the Gestapo.
In the [GERMAN].
So he said, can you stay here for a few days?
We want to talk to you.
I said, if I have to, yeah.
Well, we can put you in a hotel if I have to.
And-- and the girl said, no.
You don't have to put him in a hotel.
I can take care of him and make sure he stays here.
The next-- the next day, we started already.
But they-- it was not a deposition.
Let's say for the next two weeks I had an interrogation going on.
Yes.
In-- in a-- in a separate rooms where the prosecutor were.
Not in-- in the courtroom.
What they wanted me to do, go in the courtroom, listen to it, and tell them where they makes mistakes or what I remember, and give them the information.
I knew three of them.
Yes.
Two-- let's say three of them.
Two of the defendants.
One was Dr. iur.
Eduard Strauch.
The other one was Dr. iur.
Sandberger.
From Riga was Dr. iur.
Rudolf Lange.
They were lawyers.
And the-- one of the defendant, Heinz Jost, I only have seen once.
He was a general.
He came to Salaspils when we make the mass grave.
And he was the one when-- when the [GERMAN] was, where 5,000 Jews in Riga got killed in March 1942.
He was in charge then.
The Germans were under quite a-- a guard, and you could not go in the courtroom without a pass.
Yes.
Yes.
Lieutenant [?
Schwartz. ?]
I don't know.
He was in that same lawsuit.
Then I met a fellow named Dodd.
He was later a senator here from Connecticut.
The father.
And I broke his neck too.
He was involved in [GERMAN].
In 1964, they were closing the armory in Springfield--
--which was making weapons for the--
And then there was somebody else, but I don't know in which case he was involved.
Clancy was his name.
He was a lawyer later on in Springfield.
Case Nine.
Ohlendorf.
He admitted in the courtroom that 90,000 Jews got shot under his command.
And when Michael Musmanno, the presiding judge, ask him, what would you have done if your mother or sister would have been one of the victims?
He answered, if I had had the order, I would have shot them too.
They were very aggressive.
Yes.
Because they first saw what freedom in the courtroom means, which was never in Germany before.
Not that I know.
Not of one of them.
Musmanno was very aggressive.
He-- here from the United States.
And he was later on-- on the Pennsylvania Supreme Court.
I had no contact with.
No.
Nobody.
No contact.
The media was under control of the military government.
So there is nothing to say about--
Yeah.
I had no contact with, because I had a pass and I had to go in a special section.
That the Germans should have been treated like we were treated in a concentration camp, and not like babies.
They were treated like gentlemen in a courtroom.
I-- it didn't make sense to me.
They were criminals, but they were not treated like criminals.
I-- on and off altogether for three months.
I went out with a girl.
[LAUGHS]
She was a-- Greta [?
Wolf. ?] She was an interpreter.
And she was later on in charge of the typing pool.
But most of her time, she was interpreter and-- when the German physicians were on trial because she was a registered nurse and midwife in England.
So she knew all the medical terms.
We started to date towards this time.
I married her in July 1948.
Yeah.
In Gothenburg, Sweden.
I felt very good, but it made me sick that the Germans were let go after so few years in jail.
It did not affect me at all because-- it left me cold because I still had nightmares from it, and I still have on and off.
I felt that justice was to be done finally.
And it should have been done in a more radical way.
We went to see the theater in the evening, or we went out taking trips around Nuremberg and things like that.
There was nothing in Nuremberg I could do, except-- which was illegal for me, and I went anyway with her-- was the American restaurants and things like that.
Which was illegal for German and-- and DPs.
But I went with her anyway, because she was a member of the United States Forces at that case.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Didn't bother me.
No.
German?
None.
I had no friends in Germany.
No.
I did not feel at home there while I was in Germany.
No.
I would say yes.
Yeah.
